title: Electrochemical sample pretreatment coupled on-line with electrospray mass spectrometry for enhanced elemental analysis
abstract:
Electrochemical Sample Pretreatment Coupled
On-line with Electrospray Mass Spectrometry for
Enhanced Elemental Analysis
Jack R. Pretty and Gary J. Van Berkel*
Chemical and Analytical Sciences Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, Tennessee 37831-6365, USA
The successful coupling of a thin-layer, ﬂow-by electrochemical cell on-line with electrospray mass
spectrometry (ES-MS) for electrochemically modulated sample preconcentration and clean up, using
copper(II) as the targeted analyte, is demonstrated. Particular requirements for the interfacing of this
electrochemical system with ES-MS, the choice of cell and electrode materials, and the solvent and
electrolyte requirements are addressed. The transfer of Cu2 at low mg/L levels from matrices that cannot
be analyzed directly by ES-MS, including 100% aqueous solutions containing up to 1% (v/v) HNO3 and 1%
(w/w) NaCl, to a more suitable medium (i.e. CH3OH/H2O (95/5 v/v) with 5.0 mM ammonium acetate and
0.75% (v/v) acetic acid) via electrochemically controlled reduction (deposition) and oxidation (stripping) of
the Cu2 is demonstrated. Acidic samples and samples containing signiﬁcant levels of dissolved solids
required cell washout times of up to 4.0 min following electrochemical extraction. Nonetheless, analysis time
was still less than 8 min per sample. Quantiﬁcation of 20–50 mg/L Cu2 in various matrices using standard
additions with this electrochemical/ES-MS combination is evaluated. The limitations of this combination in
its present state of development and possible future directions are summarized. # 1998 John Wiley & Sons,
Ltd.
Received 27 August 1998; Revised 9 September 1998; Accepted 10 September 1998
Electrospray (ES) ionization is an electrostatic spray
technique that is used to assist the transfer of ionic species
present in a liquid solution into the gas phase for mass
analysis. This combination, termed electrospray mass
spectrometry (ES-MS),1 has arguably had its largest impact
in the analysis of polar, high molecular weight organics,
particularly biopolymers. ES-MS has also proved however
to be a powerful means to analyze a wide variety of small
molecules, organometallics and even inorganic species.1–3
In fact, there is a growing effort, advanced largely by
Horlick’s group,3–10 but also by others,11–21 towards the use
of ES-MS as a complement or alternative to more traditional
methods of inorganic analysis such as inductively coupled
plasma-atomic emission spectrophotometry (ICP-AES) and
ICP-MS.22 ES-MS instrumental conditions (mainly invol-
ving the atmospheric sampling interface) may be adjusted to
provide two basic modes of operation for inorganic analysis,
viz. ‘cluster-ion mode’ and ‘metal-ion mode’.6 The former
mode preserves molecular information, thereby providing
direct speciation of the elements present in solution. This
operational mode offers a complement to ICP-AES or ICP-
MS, which, because all molecular information is destroyed
during the ionization process, can provide speciation only in
an indirect fashion through coupling with a prior separation
method using appropriate standards. The metal-ion mode
offers an alternative method for elemental determination. In
this mode, extreme interface conditions are established to
break up molecular interactions so that only bare metal ions
(as is the case of ICP-AES or ICP-MS) are observed in the
mass spectrum, irrespective of the particular metal species
originally present in the solution. Particular advantages of
ES-MS in this role, compared with ICP-MS, include the less
expensive nature of the instrumentation and the ability to
accommodate easily a wide range of organic solvent
systems. Of course, ES-MS, like ICP-MS, affords isotopic
information not available from ICP-AES data. Moreover,
the ability to use ES-MS for elemental analysis, in addition
to organic and biological analysis, further increases the
versatility and overall value of this mass spectrometric
technique.
As an alternative to ICP-MS for elemental determination,
ES-MS faces a number of practical limitations making it
difficult to analyze real samples. These limitations include
the relatively poor detection limits for most inorganic
analytes (i.e. low mg/L5,10,18,20 versus low to sub ng/L for
ICP-MS22). Even more significant are solvent and matrix
effects on gas-phase ionization efficiency and the ES
process itself.23 Almost all elemental ES-MS work to date
has made use of metal salt solutions composed mainly
(>90% v/v) of organic solvents such as CH3OH or CH3CN.
In general, best gas-phase signals, regardless of the analyte,
are almost always observed with ES-MS when using
organic solvents and when a minimal amount of electrolyte
is present in solution.23 However, most real samples
presented for elemental determination will be in an aqueous
medium, often containing significant levels of acid (e.g. 1–
2% HNO3 v/v) added to promote sample dissolution or
sample stability. Such solutions pose little problem for ICP-
MS.22 As shown herein, gas-phase ion signals derived from
*Correspondence to: G. J. Van Berkel, Chemical and Analytical
Sciences Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge,
Tennessee 37831-6365, USA.
Contract/grant sponsor: Perkin-Elmer SCIEX; Contract/grant number:
CRADA No. ORNL96-0458.
Contract/grant sponsor: Division of Chemical Sciences, Ofﬁce of
Basic Energy Sciences, US Department of Energy; Contract/grant
number: DE-AC05-96OR22464.
CCC 0951–4198/98/211644–09 $17.50
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
RAPID COMMUNICATIONS IN MASS SPECTROMETRY
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 12, 1644–1652 (1998)
 metals in such solutions are severely depressed in ES-MS.
The situation is worsened with high salt matrix samples,
such as sea water,18 which also prove difficult to analyze
directly by ICP-MS.24 Not only is gas-phase ion generation
affected, but spectroscopic overlaps and plugging of the
atmospheric sampling orifice, because of the high dissolved
solids content of the sample, become concerns. With ICP-
MS, a minimum 10-fold dilution with H2O is often
necessary to enable direct analysis of such samples. With
ES-MS, dilution with organic solvents to yield a solution
with less than 10% water generally improves signal levels.25
However, to be particularly effective, mineral acid con-
centrations must be reduced by dilution to below 0.1% v/v
and other salts/electrolytes to levels of no more than 10–
20 mM. Even ‘well-behaved’ (i.e. volatile) electrolytes such
as ammonium acetate (NH4Ac) at levels of more than
20 mM can severely degrade ES-MS signals.26 In the case of
a sea water sample (ca. 3.1% salinity), this translates to a
minimum dilution of 30- to 50-fold. Thus, direct analysis of
‘real’ samples by ES-MS would appear to require at least a
10-fold dilution (e.g. ‘clean’ samples in H2O) and possibly
several times more dilution for samples in more difficult
matrices (i.e. H2O containing acid or salt). The necessity of
such extensive dilution for direct, real sample analysis
compromises the utility of ES-MS elemental determinations
because of the already modest concentration detection
levels of the technique.
On-line separation of elemental species from sample
matrices detrimental to ES-MS operation, preferably with
simultaneous preconcentration to maintain or improve
concentration detection limits, would benefit elemental
determinations with real samples. A generic scheme of this
type might involve a preconcentration device to capture the
analyte, such as a short chromatographic column, which can
be washed free of matrix and from which the analyte may be
subsequently back-flushed to the mass spectrometer via a
change in solvent composition. Such an approach is often
used in the analysis of organics by ES-MS.27 Our pre-
concentration/matrix elimination approach described here
makes use of an on-line electrochemical flow cell, in place
of the chromatographic column in this scheme, to effect
analyte retention. With this approach, it is possible to
selectively
control
the
retention
(i.e.
electrochemical
extraction/deposition) and elution (i.e. electrochemical
stripping) of a target analyte through the choice of working
electrode material and potential, rather than via solvent
switching. The analyte is retained in the present case by
electrolytic reduction, depositing the analyte (Mn) in
metallic form onto the working electrode of the cell (Eqn
(1)), while interfering matrix constituents and other non-
electroactive elements in the sample are diverted to waste.
Following deposition the analyte can be washed free of
matrix by a flow of ‘clean’ solvent through the cell. The
stripping step is accomplished by oxidation of the deposited
metal, through control of electrode potential, releasing it in
ionic form back into the solution for subsequent detection
by the mass spectrometer, Eqn (2).
Mn
solution  neÿ ! Melectrode
1
Melectrode ! Mn
solution  neÿ
2
A major benefit of this approach is the ability to liberate
the preconcentrated analyte into a matrix ideal for mass
spectrometric detection. In the case of ES-MS, this would be
a predominantly organic solvent matrix containing a
sufficient quantity of an electrolyte that affords control of
the electrochemical cell, but that does not inhibit gas-phase
ion generation. A second benefit, of course, is the signal
enhancement gained through preconcentration that main-
tains or possibly improves concentration detection limits
simultaneously with the elimination of problematic matrix
effects.
The utility of this on-line, electrochemically modulated,
approach to elemental preconcentration and cleanup has
been previously demonstrated by our group at Oak Ridge
with ICP-MS28–30 and by others with ICP-AES or ICP-
MS.31–38 In our work, using a thin-layer, flow-by electrode
cell design, we have demonstrated rapid (<90 s) elimination
of high levels of problematic matrices and simultaneous
signal enhancements (in some cases greater than 2 orders of
magnitude) with several different elements using deposition
times of 2 to 15 min. Furthermore, a dynamic range of 104
has been shown as well as excellent quantification via
standard additions. In a preliminary report, we showed that
coupling of this electrochemical methodology on-line with
ES-MS was possible using silver (I) as a test case, but real
samples and quantitative analysis were not addressed.39 In
the present paper, we more fully explore the electrochemi-
cal/ES-MS coupling for sample preconcentration and
cleanup using copper(II) as the targeted analyte. Particular
requirements for the interfacing of this electrochemical
system with ES-MS, the choice of cell and electrode
materials, and the solvent and electrolyte requirements are
addressed. The transfer of Cu2 from media wholly
unsuited to ES-MS operation to suitable media via
electrochemical control is demonstrated. The method of
standard additions was evaluated for quantification of 20–
50 mg/L Cu2 in various matrices.
EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
All solutions were prepared using HPLC grade methanol (J.
T. Baker, Phillipsburg, NJ, USA) and deionized water
(Milli-RO 12 Plus, Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). The
medium used as a carrier in the electrochemical flow system
and for most samples was CH3OH/H2O (95/5 v/v) which
contained 5.0 mM ammonium acetate (99.999% grade,
Aldrich, Milwaukee, WI, USA) and 0.75% (v/v) double
distilled PPB/Teflon grade acetic acid (Aldrich). A Cu stock
solution was prepared by diluting a 1000  3 mg/mL Cu2
standard (High Purity Standard, Baxter Scientific Products,
Charleston, SC, USA) with H2O. Ultrex Ultrapure grade
HNO3 and NaCl (J. T. Baker) were used for sample
matrices. Samples were prepared by pipetting 20 mL
aliquots of the appropriate medium into glass scintillation
vials with foil-lined caps (Kimble Glass Inc., Vineland, NJ,
USA), then adding the appropriate volume of Cu2 stock
solution. For standard addition work, the sample containing
the necessary amounts of CH3OH and/or H2O, Cu2 stock
solution, HNO3 and NaCl was prepared in 100 mL volume.
Aliquots (20 mL) of the sample were pipetted into 20 mL
vials into which the appropriate amounts of Cu2 stock
solution for spiking had already been added. The amount of
Cu2 stock solution used to prepare the original 100 mL
sample and the aliquots added to the vials were pipetted by
weight to ensure accuracy.
All ES-MS experiments were performed on an PE SCIEX
(Concord, Ontario, Canada) API 165 single quadrupole
system using a TurboIonSpray source. A 30 cm long, Teflon
encapsulated fused silica (TEFS) transfer tube (75 mm i.d.
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 12, 1644–1652 (1998)
ELECTROCHEMICAL PRETREATMENT FOR ESI-MS OF METAL IONS

All ES-MS experiments were performed on an PE SCIEX
(Concord, Ontario, Canada) API 165 single quadrupole
system using a TurboIonSpray source. A 30 cm long, Teflon
encapsulated fused silica (TEFS) transfer tube (75 mm i.d.
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 12, 1644–1652 (1998)
ELECTROCHEMICAL PRETREATMENT FOR ESI-MS OF METAL IONS
fused silica encapsulated in 1/16 in. o.d. Teflon, CETAC
Technologies, Inc., Omaha, NE, USA) connected a 3.5 cm
long stainless steel ES emitter (400 mm o.d., 100 mm i.d.) to
the stainless steel 254 mm i.d. bore-through bulkhead
grounding port built into the source assembly. This union
electrically isolated the electrochemical cell from the ES
emitter high voltage. For maximum 63Cu signal, orifice
and ring electrode voltages were set to approximately 85–
90 V and 300–325 V, respectively, and the ES emitter held
at 4.4kV. The emitter was placed 1.5–2.5 cm from the
curtain gas plate aperture and angled to spray across the
aperture. Nitrogen was used for sample nebulization and for
the heated cross-flow ‘Turbo gas’ (typically 6.5 L/min, 200–
250°C). Single ion monitoring of m/z 63 (63Cu) was
carried out using a 100 ms dwell time with a 2.0 ms pause
time between each data acquisition.
A microprocessor-controlled gas displacement pump
(GDP, pressurized with Ar) was used to pump solvent and
sample through two microprocessor actuated six-port PEEK
valves (Microneb 2000, CETAC), the cell, and on to the ES-
MS instrument at a flow rate of 60 mL/min (Fig. 1(a)).
Connections between the electrochemical flow cell and the
route valve were made using 75 mm i.d. TEFS and a 254 mm
i.d. Teflon tube (10 cm long) was used to connect the
delivery and route valves. Outlet 1 or outlet 2 of the route
valve was connected to the grounding bulkhead union via a
10 cm long, 75 mm i.d. TEFS tube. The delivery valve was
loaded by pulling sample through the loop with a peristaltic
pump (Gilson, Le Bel, France). A schematic of the thin-
layer electrochemical cell used in this work is shown in Fig.
1(b)). This cell is the same as described in detail
elsewhere,30 except that the working electrode is an offset
GC disk (6 mm dia., Bioanalytical Systems, Inc., West
Lafayette, IN, USA) positioned mid-way between the
reference electrode port and outlet port in the assembled
cell. A PAR Model 173 potentiostat with a PAR Model 175
universal programmer (Princeton Applied Research Corp.,
Princeton, NJ, USA) was used to step among the various
potentials applied to the cell during the course of an
Generally, experiments were begun with freshly polished
electrodes30 using valve configuration 1, which allowed
63Cu signals to be continuously monitored as sample
flowed through the cell. A 63.5 mg/L Cu2 standard would
be injected and cell potential adjusted to ensure no Cu2
was retained at either the working or counter electrode. The
potential was then stepped to a negative value, which was
adjusted to maximize deposition efficiency. The deposited
Cu was then stripped from the working electrode by
stepping to a sufficiently positive potential. Several runs
of this type were carried out to test and condition the system.
Typical applied potentials were: initial = 200 mV, depos-
ition = ÿ600 to ÿ800 mV, stripping = 600 mV. In actual
experiments, 2.0 min were allowed following sample
injection (delivery valve to INJ mode) to obtain a steady
state ES-MS signal before the deposition potential was
applied. After the deposition period had elapsed, the sample
delivery valve was switch to ‘load’, stopping sample flow to
the cell. A sample washout time of 2.0 min was allowed
prior to applying the stripping potential. This same
configuration was used for samples in suitable matrices or
when the steady-state continuous nebulization signal from a
sample injection was to be monitored. For high matrix
samples, valve configuration 2 was used so that sample
Figure 1. Schematic representations of the electrochemical cell/ES-MS system. (a) Pump,
dual six-port valves, and electrochemical cell flow paths shown with both valves in load
mode. Solid lines represent 254 mm i.d. TeflonTM tubing. Dotted lines represent encapsulated
fused silica tubing: 10 cm long, 75 mm i.d. tubing to cell, 10 cm long, 75 mm i.d. outlet tubing.
Valve Configuration 1: outlet 1 to waste, outlet 2 to ES-MS, route valve in inject mode at all
times. Used when necessary to monitor sample injection steady-state signals. Valve
Configuration 2: outlet 2 to ES-MS, outlet 1 to waste, route valve in inject mode for analyte
deposition, load mode for analyte stripping. Used to isolate ES-MS from sample matrix. (b)
Exploded view of electrochemical cell component parts. Note offset configuration of the
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 12, 1644–1652 (1998)
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
ELECTROCHEMICAL PRETREATMENT FOR ESI-MS OF METAL IONS
flowing through the cell during deposition and washout
could be diverted to waste, protecting the ES-MS interface.
Washout times were extended to as much as 4.0 min for acid
A number of factors are important to the successful coupling
and practicality of the electrochemically based sample
preconcentration and clean up/ES-MS combination. The
majority of these factors are the same as those we previously
addressed in coupling this same thin-layer flow cell system
on-line with ICP-MS.29 In general, one must operate under
conditions that optimize electrolytic performance of the cell
(both deposition and stripping), minimize matrix washout
times, minimize dispersion of the stripping peak as analyte
is released from the cell, and maximize sample throughput.
Previous experience dictates that optimum cell perfor-
mance, in terms of analyte deposition, requires a flow rate in
the range of 50–100 mL/min, a large working electrode, and
close spacing of the cell electrodes. Slower flow rates,
although they may increase the electrolytic deposition of the
cell (higher flows decrease efficiency) and to some degree
signal levels in ES-MS, significantly extend matrix washout
times and reduce sample throughout. Flow rates of 50–
100 mL/min are easily accessible with a pneumatically
nebulized ES source. Choosing a flow rate of 60 mL/min, we
found that signal levels could be further enhanced with the
use of a cross-flow heated gas (i.e. TurboIonSpray) to aid
desolvation, particularly when the solvent contained H2O
Our cell was fitted with the largest GC working electrode
possible in the current design (i.e. a 6.0 mm dia. disk),
narrow bore-through inlet and outlet fittings (254 mm i.d.),
and a spacing between working and counter electrode of
16 mm (1.1 mL cell volume). The large electrode and close
spacing of the electrodes resulted in a cell with a high
electrode surface-to-cell volume ratio, and therefore, a high
deposition efficiency. The low cell volume also proves
crucial for efficient preconcentration. The degree of
preconcentration attainable is determined ultimately by
the ratio of the volume from which the analyte is extracted
to the volume into which the analyte is stripped. In the
particular cell design used here, the working electrode was
partly offset from the counter electrode. This was done to
minimize possible redeposition of material stripped from
the working electrode at the counter electrode as it flowed
Maximum stripping peak height requires that the peak
dispersion be minimized upon travel from the cell to the
mass spectrometer. Therefore, short, narrow-bore transfer
lines were used to connect the cell to the switching valves
and to the mass spectrometer. To avoid problems owing to
narrow tubing, which is most problematic with samples of
high dissolved solids content, all transfer lines were 75 mm
Of greater consideration for coupling the electrochemical
cell with ES-MS was the electrolyte/solvent system. In ICP-
MS, a H2O/HNO3 (99/1 v/v) electrolyte/solvent system
worked well in terms of gas-phase ion generation and also
provided sufficient conductivity for control of the electro-
chemical cell.28,29,30 Moreover, metals were quite soluble in
this solvent, and therefore, cell and transfer line washout
was efficient and rapid. Gas-phase ion generation in ES-MS
is much more susceptible to solvent and matrix than in ICP-
MS.22,23 The amount of acid and H2O must be minimized in
the solvent system used with ES-MS for maximum
generation of gas-phase metal ions (see below). Organic
solvents, such as CH3OH and/or CH3CN might be the best
choices for the major component of the solvent system as
they normally provide for good metal ion generation in ES-
MS.25 They are also usually suitable for electrochemical
experiments.40 To provide adequate solubility of metals,
sample stability and rapid washout from the cell and transfer
lines, some small amount of H2O and an acid must be
present in the solvent system, as well as an electrolyte to
provide sufficient solvent conductivity. Particularly suitable
candidates for the acid and the electrolyte are acetic acid
(HAc) and ammonium acetate (NH4Ac). When present in
solvents at or below 1.0% (v/v) and 20 mM, respectively,
they pose no problem in organic ES-MS analysis.26
After an exploration of a variety of CH3OH/H2O and
CH3CN/H2O combinations, with NH4Ac and/or 0.75% HAc
added as electrolyte, we settled upon CH3OH/H2O (95/5
v/v) containing 5.0 mM NH4Ac and 0.75% HAc by volume.
This system enabled proper operation of the electrochemical
cell, and provided excellent signal for the targeted analyte,
viz. copper, and relatively short analyte washout times. We
found that corrosion of the platinum counter electrode took
place during the deposition stage of the experiments when
CH3CN was a component of the solvent system. Therefore,
this solvent was avoided given our current cell design.
The targeted analyte, copper (II), although present as
Cu2 in solution, was detected as Cu in the gas phase. As
originally described by Kebarle’s group,41 the desolvation
process for Cu2 and other multiply charged metals often
results in charge reduction through transfer of charge to a
solvent molecule/cluster. The data in Fig. 2 show the signal
63Cu (m/z 63) recorded from a blank
injection and the lowest concentration Cu2 standards from
a series of large volume injections ranging in concentration
from 0.635 mg/L to 63.5 mg/L. (Note that in this solvent
system an isobaric overlap was present at m/z 65, probably
the proton bound dimer of CH3OH, that prohibited the use
of the 65Cu isotope. This copper specific overlap might be
overcome if necessary with the use of CD3OH as a solvent
or by using tandem mass spectrometry to dissociate the
dimer.) The standards were prepared in and injected into our
‘ideal’ solvent system comprised of 95/5 (v/v) CH3OH/H2O
with 5.0 mM NH4Ac and 0.75% (v/v) HAc. From this flow
injection data set one notes that it might be possible to just
distinguish at plateau signal levels the presence of Cu above
background at the 0.635 mg/L level. From the calibration
plot (not shown), which was linear (r = 0.99984) over this
two orders of magnitude range, the detection limit (DL) was
estimated (3sx/y/slope) to be 1.4 mg/L. This level is
consistent with the low mg/L DLs reported by others in
elemental ES-MS.5,10,18,20 It is worth noting that the
linearity of response for 63Cu as a function of concentra-
tion did not require the ratio technique used by Horlick and
co-workers.5 Horlick and co-workers found that the ratio of
the analyte signal to another ion in the mass spectrum,
plotted as a function of analyte concentration, yields a
straight line calibration. Our linear response for 63Cu
(albeit over just two orders of magnitude, but at very low
level) probably results from the high electrolyte concentra-
tion. In such a case, analyte concentration has a much
smaller influence on the overall ES process.42 Moreover,
this result is consistent with the ability to obtain linear
response as a function of analyte concentration in the

ELECTROCHEMICAL PRETREATMENT FOR ESI-MS OF METAL IONS
analysis of organics by electrospray. In such cases, buffers
and/or other electrolytes are also usually present in solution
The data in Fig. 3(a) includes the ES-MS 63Cu signal
level recorded with a 31.8 mg/L Cu2 sample prepared in
our ‘ideal’ solvent discussed above (CH3OH/H2O (95/5 v/v)
containing 5.0 mM NH4Ac and 0.75% HAc by volume)
during continuous sample nebulization. The remaining data
illustrate the detrimental effect on this signal caused by
increasing the amount of H2O at the expense of CH3OH in
the solvent. Increasing the H2O content of the solvent by
just 5% reduced signals to about 65% of their original
levels. Signal levels further decreased to about 30% in 50%
H2O, reaching a level just under 25% of the original value in
100% H2O. The loss of signal attributable to the large
percentage of H2O in the solvent system was exacerbated
upon the addition of 0.1% HNO3 (v/v) as the data in Fig.
3(b) show. In this example, Cu2 levels were increased to
635 mg/L so that it would be possible to observe 63Cu in the
acid containing sample. Compared to our ideal solvent, we
observed a signal level of only 1.5% when spraying from
H2O/HNO3 (99.9/0.1 v/v). Clearly, this latter solvent system
would be problematic for analysis of metals by ES-MS. Yet,
it contains just 10% of the HNO3 concentration of a typical
analytical metals standard. A sample of this latter composi-
tion would have to be diluted extensively (by a factor >10)
to a give a solvent composition closer to that of our ideal
solvent and with a HNO3 concentration well below 0.1% v/v
to have any hope of getting the same sensitivity as obtained
with our ideal solvent. Of course, dilution lowers the
concentration of analyte in the sample, and therefore,
compromises the utility of the technique for low level
quantitative analysis, raising detection levels by the same
factor as the level of dilution.
The problem of compromised detection posed by dilution
or the presence of a metal in a matrix wholly unsuited for
ES-MS analysis may be addressed by the on-line electro-

ELECTROCHEMICAL PRETREATMENT FOR ESI-MS OF METAL IONS
chemical pretreatment approach presented here. As a first
example to illustrate the basic experiment, the results from
three successive electrochemical/ES-MS experiments with
a 31.8 mg/L Cu2 standard are shown in Fig. 4. In this case,
the Cu2 is deposited/extracted from Eqn (1) and stripped
into Eqn (2), our ideal solvent/electrolyte system. A metal
sample of similar solution composition might result, for
example, if an aqueous metal sample were diluted with a
large volume of CH3OH with NH4Ac and HAc added at the
appropriate concentrations. The plot of
intensity in Fig. 4(a) is a blow up of the first of the 3
replicate experiments shown in Fig. 4(b). Valve configura-
tion 1 was used in this case so that analyte passed
continuously through the cell and on to the mass spec-
trometer (see Experimental and Fig. 1 for details). The
timing sequence correlating (I) sample injection, (II) analyte
deposition, (III) cell washout, and (IV) analyte stripping
with applied electrode potential is shown along with the
various terms used to define system performance. The
minimum continuous nebulization steady-state signal re-
corded during sample injection, with a deposition potential
applied was termed DS. The maximum continuous neb-
ulization signal recorded during sample injection, without a
deposition potential applied was termed CS, and the
stripping peak height was termed PH. Percent deposition
efficiency was defined as DE = [(CS-DS)/CS] 100 and the
signal enhancement factor as EF = PH/CS. In this particular
example, DE was 66% and EF was 31 for just a 2.0 min
deposition time. The actual DE (and therefore stripping
peak height and EF) varied slightly from day-to-day as a
result of electrode condition. In general, for a fixed flow rate
and optimized deposition potential, the EF could be
increased by increasing deposition time as we have
previously demonstrated in our ICP-MS work.29,30 The 3
replicate analyses in Fig. 4(b) illustrate the excellent
reproducibility in PH (2.3% RSD) that was obtained in
these experiments and the relatively rapid sample through-
put (ca. 8 min/sample). It is worth noting that the EF = 31
achieved for a 2.0 min deposition time with Cu2 was
comparable to the best case achieved with silver(I) using our
cell on-line with the ICP-MS, even though a DE > 85% was
commonly achieved under similar conditions for Ag.29
Unlike the conventional ICP-MS systems we used, ES-MS
does not use a spray chamber for droplet desolvation.
Dispersion of the stripping peak in the spray chamber
contributes to the broadening of the stripping peak, and
results in a lower PH. Thus, in ES-MS, for the same amount
of material deposited, the PH height (and also EF) may be
greater than that achieved with an ICP-MS equipped with a
spray chamber, because of lesser stripping peak dispersion.
The data in Fig. 5 illustrate how the electrochemical/ES-
MS combination may be used to enhance low level signals.
These plots for 63Cu resulted from two experiments, the
first with 0.635 mg/L Cu2, the other with a blank, both
samples being prepared in our ideal solvent system. A
10 min deposition time and valve configuration 1 were used.
Note that it was not readily possible to distinguish among
the continuous nebulization signal levels for the sample and
blank. This sample level is the same as the lowest level
shown in Fig. 2 which was above the blank level by no more
than 100–200 cps. Conversely, the difference between the
stripping peak heights for the 0.635 mg/L Cu2 sample and
the blank was about 4000 cps. (The stripping peak for the
blank results from low level Cu2 contamination present in
the solvent system.) Thus, the levels of Cu2 within the
sample and blank are easily distinguished using the
electrochemical preconcentration/ES-MS procedure. One
can therefore anticipate that the concentration DL for Cu2
in this solvent system might be lowered via this preconcen-
tration approach. Thus, the degradation of DLs resulting
from dilution of the originally aqueous sample with organic
The alternative sample analysis scenario would be to
handle an aqueous sample, possibly containing an acid and/
or dissolved solids, without dilution with an organic solvent.

a H2O/HNO3 (99/1 v/v) sample that also contained 1% w/w
NaCl. Neither of these samples could be readily analyzed
directly by ES-MS. The H2O/HNO3 matrix severely
63Cu gas-phase ion signal. The NaCl
inhibits ion generation as well, but also contributes back-
ground noise and rapidly contaminates or even plugs the
atmospheric sampling orifice of the mass spectrometer. In
each of the electrochemical/ES-MS experiments shown, the
Cu2 was deposited from the matrix solvent, the cell washed

free of matrix by a carrier solution of our ideal composition,
then subsequently stripped into this ideal solvent for
detection downstream by the mass spectrometer. For these
experiments valve configuration 2 was used so that the mass
spectrometer could be isolated from the matrix components
(which are diverted to waste) during the deposition and
washout portions of the experiments. As such, no contin-
uous nebulization signal owing to the analyte solution was
observed during the time sample travelled through the cell.
As the plots in Fig. 6 also show, when dealing with
sample matrices containing acid or salt, we observed that
background levels during the time period in which the
solution travels through the cell and to the mass spec-
trometer, following cell washout (e.g. the time window
between points I and II in Fig. 6(a) and (b)), varied from
those levels observed when the cell was by-passed. In Fig.
6(a), for example, the background levels are lower during
this time and in Fig. 6(b), the levels are higher. The
variations in background levels were found to be a function
of both the solvent matrix and the potentials applied and
may not actually be 63Cu. However, because the back-
ground level variations were consistent among matrix-
matched samples, they posed no problems for analytical
determinations. Indeed, the data in Fig. 6 demonstrate that it
is possible with our electrochemical/ES-MS preconcentra-
tion/clean up combination to selectively remove a metal
from matrices totally unsuited for ES-MS analysis, transfer
it into an ideal solvent system, and detect it with excellent
signal-to-background levels. Moreover, this can be accom-
plished reproducibly in a relatively rapid fashion (5–10 min
We have also investigated the ability to perform
quantitative analysis with this combination, by means of
standard additions, using Cu2 standards prepared in four
different solvent matrices. Figure 7 shows the standard
addition plots obtained for Cu2 in three of these matrices.

In the first case (Fig. 7(a)), the sample (unspiked) contained
18.8 mg/L Cu2 dissolved in our ideal solvent/electrolyte
system. Three spiked solutions, chosen to cover a range up
to about two times the concentration of Cu2 in the original
sample, were also analyzed. As mentioned previously, a
sample solution of similar composition might result from
dilution of an aqueous sample with CH3OH/electrolyte prior
to analysis. This standard addition plot is fairly linear
(r = 0.99152), but a wide 95% confidence interval indicates
the level of imprecision. Nonetheless, the true value,
18.8 mg/L Cu2, and experimentally determined value,
(26  14) mg/L Cu2 agree within the 95%CL. The results
of two other standard addition experiments attempted are
plotted in Fig. 7(b). These two experiments were carried out
with unspiked standards of 18.8 mg/L Cu2 in H2O
containing 5.0 mM NH4Ac/0.75% (v/v) HAc and 34.5 mg/
L Cu2 in H2O containing only 1% HNO3 (v/v) as the
electrolyte, respectively. The values for the respective
spikes were again chosen to range up to about 2 times the
concentration of Cu2 in the sample. The linearity and
precision of both of these standard addition plots are better
than that obtained in the previous example. Moreover, the
experimentally determined value for Cu2 in the H2O/
NH4AC/HAc matrix sample, (17.8  6.4) mg/L, was quite
accurate. Unfortunately, in the case of the H2O/HNO3
matrix, although the experimentally determined value was
fairly precise (27.2  5.0) mg/L, it was below the true value
of 34.5 mg/L by about 21%. We also obtained a linear and
precise standard addition plot for a 34.5 mg/L Cu2 standard
prepared in H2O containing 1% (w/w) NaCl, 5.0 mM
NH4Ac and 0.75% (v/v) HAc. In this case, the experimen-
tally determined value was approximately two times higher
than the true value. The possible sources of these
In the results presented here, we have shown that an
electrochemical preconcentration and sample clean up
system may be coupled on-line with ES-MS and that it
may be used to enhance the analysis of metals by ES-MS. In
fact, these results demonstrate the possibility of using ES-
MS for the analysis of metals in matrices that heretofore
could not be accomplished. This hybrid combination allows
for both signal enhancement via preconcentration and the
elimination of sample matrices detrimental to ES-MS
operation. By means of this electrochemical extraction
and stripping approach metals may be transferred via
electrochemical control from matrices that cannot be
analyzed directly by ES-MS to another solvent system that
is optimum for metals detection.
Quantification with this electrochemical/ES-MS combi-
nation via standard additions was shown to be possible.
However, quantification when analyzing samples in ma-
trices containing high concentrations of acid and/or salt, in
particular, will need to be improved.
Although not attempted, it should also be possible to
quantify via a calibration curve if one can match standard
and sample matrices. For example, we have found it
possible to obtain linear plots for stripping peak heights as a
function of Cu2 concentration, using a fixed deposition
time, over a 1 order of magnitude concentration range. The
actual linear dynamic range achievable has yet to be
explored, but might be expected to be at least several orders
of magnitude given our previous results with the electro-
chemical/ICP-MS combination.28,29,30 To more fully judge
the quantification aspects of this electrochemical/ES-MS
scheme for metals analysis, future work will need to involve
certified standard solutions, internal standards, other/multi-
ple elements, and possibly isotope dilution.
As previously mentioned, CH3CN proved unsuitable as
the organic portion of our sample medium because
degradation of the platinum counter electrode occurred
when negative potentials were applied to the working
electrode. Brief experiments with a modified cell demon-
strated that use of a GC counter electrode will permit the use
of CH3CN. At a minimum, use of this solvent will allow a
wider range of applied potentials to be used than possible
with CH3OH and will also provide a cleaner background
While metals have been the subject of this paper, we
expect that this same basic electrochemical preconcentra-
tion and clean up approach may be extended to the analysis
of organic and biological analytes for which ES-MS is
ideally suited. An extensive array of electrochemical
literature describes methods for preconcentration and
detection of organic and biologicals via ‘stripping analy-
sis’,44 which might be used as a starting point to expand the
present methodology in that direction.
